steps.text: Direct questions regarding appropriate collaborative activities to the Office of the General Counsel (OGC), CDC Branch, Department of Health & Human Services (HHS).
Refer questions and issues relating to gifts to individual employees from organizations outside the federal government to the Human Resources Office (HRO), CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity.
Deposit the money in the general fund of the Treasury as soon as practicable.

Determine whether acceptance of a gift would compromise or appear to compromise the integrity of CDC or any of its employees, require CDC to undertake activities unrelated to its mission, or exert influence over its priorities.
Consider the identity of the immediate donor and, if applicable, the identity of any entity that funds the donor.
Request assistance from the OGC.
Prepare letters of acceptance for a conditional gift.
Propose project ideas to the CDC Foundation.
Cultivate partnerships around program opportunities.
Accept and utilize any gift, donation, bequest, or devise of real or personal property from the CDC Foundation.
Transfer any income from lease or hire to OFR within 24 hours of receipt for deposit with the Secretary of the Treasury.
Transfer proceeds from the liquidation of personal property to OFR within 24 hours after receipt for deposit with the Secretary of the Treasury.
Request that the executor liquidate the assets and make distribution in cash.
Forward copies of wills and trusts naming CDC as beneficiary and letters of transmittal to OGC.
Acknowledge receipt of the will or trust promptly.
Notify the Deputy Chief Financial Officer (CFO) when the estate is settled.
Follow the guidelines used to accept a monetary gift.
Enter proceeds from wills or trusts in the accounts.
Deposit and administer funds received by or for the United States Government in United States Government accounts.
Conduct conflict of interest review at CDC for all gifts subject to this policy.
Conduct a separate, independent conflict of interest review by the CDC Foundation for CDC Foundation gifts.
Apply the same standard for vetting donors for projects managed at the CDC Foundation, even when no funds come to CDC.
Conduct center-level conflict of interest review, documenting the review, the donor, and a description of the in-kind gift.
Consider what the monetary value of the gift would be, if possible, and record that value in review documentation.
Consult with OGC.
Request a CDC COI Panel Review if further elevation is required.
Consult with OGC regarding potential conflict of interest concern
Conduct an abbreviated conflict-of-interest review including review from OGC and the Ethics Desk of the EOC
Complete a Gift Acceptance Validity Survey (Appendix 2)
Relay further considerations back to OGC, the Ethics Desk, and the CDC Foundation
Perform an extra layer of scrutiny including completion of the Gift Acceptance Validity Survey (Appendix 2) before accepting the gift
Review recommendations regarding excluded sources made by the Advisory Committee to the Director in its May 2016 report, in collaboration with OGC.
Contact OFR, Office of Debt Management, for the disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts to the agency.
Include written notification to accompany all gift returns.
Contact the CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity for guidance regarding the disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts to individual employees.
Return tangible items to the donor
Pay the donor market value (retail cost of an item or service of like quality)
Destroy, share within the office, or donate the items
Advise donors from commercial organizations that the acceptance of a gift does not constitute an implied or direct endorsement
Examine the precise terms of the statute authorizing the agency to accept the gift to evaluate the propriety of a proposed use of gift funds.
Determine that the proposed use will not conflict with the terms of the agency's authorizing statute.
Follow limitations imposed by that statute.
Obtain pre-approval from OFR for gift funds expenditures for entertainment.
Ensure the expenditure meets the necessary expense test.
Determine if entertainment is necessary or essential to CDC's purposes based on the particular facts and circumstances.
Spend gift funds in the same manner as appropriated funds.
Consult with the OFR or the Ethics and Compliance Activity Office for assistance with sponsored travel.
Determine whether unsolicited grants or other types of awards are intended for CDC or for the employee serving in an individual capacity.
Accept the funds as a gift and deposit them into the appropriate CDC gift fund account.
Seek guidance as necessary from the CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity to avoid the potential for conflict of interest or other impropriety.
Seek guidance from OGC to ensure that conditions set out in grant award documents and agreements are legally appropriate.
Notify OFR of changes in the status of property that should be reflected in the general ledger.
Assign monetary or gifts in kind received by the Office of the Director to the referenced CIO for coordinated handling.
Handle the responsibilities as outlined for CIOs in section 4.B.
Forward cash or cash equivalents for deposit to the Deputy Chief Financial Officer, accompanied by a brief memorandum stating that the gift is pre-decisional and any accompanying documentation.
Mail gift fund checks to the designated USPS or Private Carrier address.
Transmit wired funds via pay.gov.
OFR deposits the gift in a suspense account.
Accept gifts less than $500 without CDC Conflict of Interest Panel Review, after performing a balancing test within OFR to determine that the donor and gift are an acceptable source.
Acknowledge the donor's agreement to transfer remaining funds of $1,000 or less to the unconditional gift account in the letter of acceptance.
Contact the donor to obtain permission in writing to use any amount remaining exceeding the $1,000 threshold.
Coordinate establishing a CAN for the gift.

Draft the letter of acceptance from CDC to the donor, stating only that the monetary gift is being deposited into the 'component's gift fund account' and stating the purpose for which the funds will be used.
Deposit the money into the gift fund account.
Record the gift as 'conditional' or 'unconditional' and code it with the appropriate program segment code.
Promptly return any uncashed checks tendered by the donor to the donor.
Draw a refund check from the Department of the Treasury.
Return uncashed checks to the donor, or draw a refund check from the Department of the Treasury if the check has already been deposited.
Thank the donor and inform them that the donation will be considered for acceptance and they will be advised of a decision at a later timeframe.
Notify the Logistics Management Branch, OSSAM, of proposals for personal property gifts, including description, accountable area, and purpose.
Submit a copy of the document accepting the gift to OSSAM.
Create a basic record in the on-line property system, including estimated value, manufacturer's serial number, month/year received, physical location, CDC decal number, and purpose.
Determine at least annually if there will be excess funds not used within a 12-month period.
Request in writing that the Chief Financial Officer invest the excess funds in interest-bearing obligations of the United States, including information on when investments will be redeemed.
Include information in the notification concerning when investments will be redeemed to meet planned outlays.
Include the amount of funds invested, the source of funds, and any recommendations regarding purchase of U.S. Treasury bills, notes, or bonds in the director's memorandum.
OFR: Provide CIOs with quarterly status sheets of all invested funds.
Upon liquidation of investments, return the monies to the same gift fund account to be used for the benefit of the CIOs.
Align the spend plan with the investment and redemption of the securities.
CDC: Conduct the Gift Acceptance Validity Survey to determine whether the interest in accepting the gift outweighs potential unfavorable concerns.
Consult the CDC Ethics and Compliance Activity and the Office of the General Counsel as a precautionary measure.
Complete the CDC Gift Pre-Acceptance Checklist.
Obtain the signature of the Recommending Official on the CDC Gift Pre-Acceptance Checklist.
Obtain the signature of the Approving Official on the CDC Gift Pre-Acceptance Checklist.
Draft the Letter of Acceptance for Conditional Gifts using the sample template.

preconditions.text: An official or agent of the Government receives money for the Government from an outside source, and the funds do not constitute an authorized repayment or the agency lacks specific statutory authority to retain the funds.
When utilizing gift acceptance authority and determining whether to accept a gift
There is a question determining whether a gift is conditional or unconditional.
In the opinion of OGC, there is a valid question about the classification of the gift, deeming it conditional.
A bequest or legacy is all or part of a general estate of a deceased individual.
Proceeds from wills or trusts, either revocable or irrevocable, are realized.
If a CIO makes a determination that there is a potential conflict of interest concern
The Emergency Operations Center (EOC) is activated for a public health emergency
There is a potential Prohibited Source, or there is need for additional review during an emergency response
Gifts are offered by a prohibited source (whether a CDC Foundation gift or a direct gift)
There is any question that a potential donor may be an excluded source
The disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts is to the agency
The disposition of prohibited or unwanted gifts is to individual employees
If a gift cannot be accepted
Subject to OGC approval as a legally permissible use of appropriated funds
Subject to approval of a supervisor or organization head
If donor is from a commercial organization
The donor does not express, directly or indirectly, an expectation of receiving a future benefit.
The gift conditions do not create a conflict of interest.
The entertainment furthers a valid, authorized function of CDC, the function could not be otherwise accomplished without the expenditure, and the expenditure does not violate donor restrictions.
Unsolicited grants or other types of awards are made to CDC employees for scientific or other professional purposes.
An employee receives funds from any outside organization that supports the costs of CDC activities.
A CIO is not specified for a gift received by the Office of the Director
The gift is cash or cash equivalents.
The gift is a check.
The gift is wired funds.
The gift is not officially accepted within the 24-hour period.

For conditional monetary gifts, the potential donor must agree in writing that upon completion of the stipulated conditions, the remaining funds of $1,000 or less will be transferred to the unconditional gift account.
If the remaining amount exceeds the $1,000 threshold.
Once approval is obtained.
If the monetary gift is accepted.
If the monetary gift is not accepted, or the donor refuses to accept CDC terms.
If the donor's check has already been deposited.
Upon receipt of the personal property gift
Upon notification of acceptance of the gift by the CIO property custodian
If it is determined that there are excess funds and the amount of these funds is $50,000 or more
Gift funds must be received prior to obligation or expenditure.
Your answer was 'no' to any questions 1 through 7, or 'yes' to any questions 8 through 18.

constraints.text: Gifts from foreign governments or international organizations, if authorized by the agency, are subject to the Foreign Gifts and Decorations Act (5 U.S.C. Section 7342).
Gifts to individual employees from organizations outside the federal government are subject to the government-wide ethics regulations.

Do not accept gifts if the conditions imposed by the donor are illegal, contrary to public policy, unreasonable to administer, contrary to generally accepted public standards, or present an actual or apparent conflict of interest.

Donor-imposed conditions must not be illegal, contrary to public policy, unreasonable to administer, contrary to generally accepted public standards, or represent an actual or apparent conflict of interest.
Gift funds must be collected in advance of use.
Proceeds from wills or trusts, either revocable or irrevocable, will not be entered in the accounts until realized.
Funds received by or for the United States Government or its agencies may not be deposited with or otherwise administered by other persons or organizations.
An outside organization (including a foundation supporting CDC) may not serve as a financial intermediary for a third party in the donation of funds, equipment, supplies, or other resources.
Neither the gift nor any conditions associated with the gift should exert undue influence over CDC programs and priorities.
A gift must not be conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be met from the gift itself or from the income of the gift.

A prohibited source is any individual or entity that: Is seeking official action by CDC; Does business or seeks to do business with CDC; Conducts activities regulated by CDC; Has interests that may be substantially affected by performance or nonperformance of an employee's official duties; Benefits from work performed by CDC, such that they can use it to promote their business; Is an organization, a majority of whose members are described in Section VI. I. 2 (5 C.F.R. Section 2635.203(d)).
Written notification must accompany all gift returns.
Employees may use first-class mail for the limited purpose of forwarding reimbursements to donors.

CDC will not accept a gift that is conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be covered by the gift itself or from income earned from the gift, unless an Act of Congress has approved such expenditure.
Gifts may not be accepted if the conditions placed on the gift create a conflict of interest.
Gifts tendered in order to secure an implied or direct endorsement are not accepted.
CDC officials cannot accept conditional gifts of real property; gifts of real property may only be accepted by the Secretary, HHS/ASFR.
Gift funds can only be used to further authorized agency purposes and in accordance with the terms of the donation.
The employee’s total salary must remain consistent with applicable statutes and regulations governing federal employee compensation.
It is recommended that only the salary of term employees be paid using gift funds.
Donated funds may not be used for entertainment that does not bear a legitimate official purpose, such as an office holiday party.
Sponsored travel is not covered by this policy.
All statutory requirements regarding the acceptance of conditional gifts must be met.
Grant funds must be used to advance CDC’s mission, and there must be no legal objections to CDC’s acceptance of these grants.
Such a gift should not be a gift to the employee.

CDC policy prohibits employees, either directly or through a third party, from requesting or suggesting donations to CDC or any of its components, except grant funds as authorized by the Comptroller General.
Must forward within 24 hours of receipt in accordance with Cash Management Policy, CDC-FM-2009-02.
Gifts from the CDC Foundation or any other donor require both a letter of acceptance and a check in order for a new CAN to be established.
Funds cannot be committed or obligated until the CIO is notified that the Office of Budget, OCFO, has approved budget authority and a CAN.
Do not reference specific names like 'Breast Cancer Gift Fund' or 'Emphysema Gift Fund' in the letter of acceptance.
If an investment is redeemed before the maturity date, there may be loss of principal.
Invested gift funds and income from these investments must retain the same limitations, limits, and restrictions as the original gifts.
Careful scrutiny should be given to any unfavorable responses.
Use CIO letterhead if the CIO Director is the signature authority.
